title: MBLEx D3 Pathology, Contraindications & Special Populations v4 (prompt-locked, aligned distractors)

questions.prompt: Mid-session, a client develops crushing chest pain, cold sweat, and shortness of breath. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Continue with lighter pressure and reassess in 15 minutes.
choices.B: Have the client drink water and walk slowly around the room.
choices.C: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).
choices.D: Finish only neck and shoulder work to reduce stress.

questions.answer: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).
questions.why: This symptom cluster suggests a possible cardiac emergency, which is outside massage scope and needs immediate medical intervention. Ending care and calling emergency services is the safest response.
questions.trap: Waiting to reassess can delay lifesaving treatment.

questions.prompt: At intake, a client reports one-sided calf swelling and tenderness after a recent long car ride. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Deeply massage the calf to improve circulation.
choices.B: Do not massage the leg and refer for urgent medical evaluation for possible DVT.
choices.C: Apply heat to the area and recheck at the end of session.
choices.D: Continue full-body massage but skip direct calf pressure.

questions.answer: Do not massage the leg and refer for urgent medical evaluation for possible DVT.
questions.why: Unilateral calf swelling and tenderness after prolonged immobility are DVT red flags and require urgent referral. Massage could increase embolic risk and is contraindicated.
questions.trap: Skipping direct calf pressure still fails to address a possible clot.

questions.prompt: A client has fever and a rapidly spreading red streak on the forearm. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Perform light lymphatic strokes around the area.
choices.B: Continue treatment while wearing gloves.
choices.C: Use cold packs and complete the planned session.
choices.D: Do not treat and refer for same-day medical evaluation for possible acute infection.

questions.answer: Do not treat and refer for same-day medical evaluation for possible acute infection.
questions.why: Fever plus spreading erythema can indicate active infection requiring medical care beyond massage scope. Treating through it may worsen spread and delay needed care.
questions.trap: Gloves do not make an unstable systemic condition safe for massage.

questions.prompt: A client presents with a painful unilateral blistering rash in a dermatomal pattern consistent with active shingles. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Postpone massage and avoid skin contact until lesions are crusted and medically cleared.
choices.B: Massage surrounding tissue deeply to reduce nerve pain.
choices.C: Cover the rash and continue full-session massage.
choices.D: Apply moist heat over lesions before treatment.

questions.answer: Postpone massage and avoid skin contact until lesions are crusted and medically cleared.
questions.why: Active shingles can be contagious and painful, and direct massage over involved tissue is inappropriate. Deferral and medical guidance protect both client and therapist.
questions.trap: Covering lesions does not remove transmission and symptom-flare risk.

questions.prompt: Before treatment, a client has blood pressure 186/112 with severe headache and blurred vision. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Proceed with relaxation massage and monitor symptoms.
choices.B: Use deep neck work to reduce blood pressure quickly.
choices.C: Have the client rest for 20 minutes, then continue if improved.
choices.D: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).

questions.answer: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).
questions.why: Markedly elevated blood pressure with neurologic symptoms may indicate hypertensive emergency and requires immediate medical care. Massage should not continue in this presentation.
questions.trap: A rest-and-recheck approach can delay urgent treatment.

questions.prompt: At 24 weeks pregnant, a client becomes dizzy and nauseated when lying supine. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Keep the client supine and lower pressure.
choices.B: Switch to side-lying with bolstering and continue only if symptoms fully resolve.
choices.C: Turn the client prone immediately to improve circulation.
choices.D: Apply abdominal compression to reduce discomfort.

questions.answer: Switch to side-lying with bolstering and continue only if symptoms fully resolve.
questions.why: Supine hypotensive symptoms in pregnancy require immediate positional modification and close monitoring. Side-lying support is a standard safety adaptation within massage scope.
questions.trap: Continuing supine can worsen vena cava compression symptoms.

questions.prompt: During a session, a client with diabetes becomes shaky, sweaty, and mildly confused. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Continue massage because these signs are usually anxiety.
choices.B: Stop treatment, give fast-acting carbohydrate if the client is alert, and monitor response.
choices.C: Have the client sleep for 20 minutes, then resume treatment.
choices.D: Use brisk friction to increase circulation and alertness.

questions.answer: Stop treatment, give fast-acting carbohydrate if the client is alert, and monitor response.
questions.why: These are possible hypoglycemia signs requiring immediate safety management before any further bodywork. If symptoms persist or worsen, escalate to emergency care.
questions.trap: Treating through confusion can allow rapid deterioration.

questions.prompt: A client has an open, draining skin lesion near the treatment area. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Avoid direct contact with the wound, use standard precautions, and modify or defer local work.
choices.B: Massage directly over the dressing to reduce edema.
choices.C: Remove the dressing to inspect tissue before massage.
choices.D: Apply oil and deep pressure across wound margins.

questions.answer: Avoid direct contact with the wound, use standard precautions, and modify or defer local work.
questions.why: Open or draining lesions are a local contraindication and require infection-control judgment and scope-appropriate modification. Direct work can increase contamination and tissue irritation.
questions.trap: Working through a dressing still risks contamination and delayed healing.

questions.prompt: A client on warfarin reports frequent bruising and requests deep tissue to the thighs. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Deep tissue is safe if bruising is from minor bumps.
choices.B: Use lighter pressure, avoid aggressive techniques, and monitor for bruising or pain.
choices.C: Use percussion over bruised spots to clear pooled blood.
choices.D: Increase pressure gradually until tissue resistance decreases.

questions.answer: Use lighter pressure, avoid aggressive techniques, and monitor for bruising or pain.
questions.why: Anticoagulation raises bleeding and hematoma risk, so pressure and technique must be modified conservatively. This keeps care within safe massage parameters.
questions.trap: Gradually increasing to deep pressure can still injure fragile tissue.

questions.prompt: A client with diabetic peripheral neuropathy has markedly reduced sensation in both feet. What is the BEST approach?

choices.A: Use deep pressure because reduced sensation lowers discomfort.
choices.B: Use very hot stones to improve nerve signaling.
choices.C: Use conservative pressure, check feedback frequently, and avoid intense thermal input.
choices.D: Skip sensory questions and proceed with normal foot protocol.

questions.answer: Use conservative pressure, check feedback frequently, and avoid intense thermal input.
questions.why: Reduced protective sensation increases risk of unnoticed pressure or thermal injury during treatment. Frequent check-ins and conservative dosing are safest.
questions.trap: Less sensation does not mean tissues can tolerate more force or heat.

questions.prompt: A 72-year-old client with osteoporosis and chronic steroid use requests vigorous back work. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Use rapid percussion over the thoracic spine to stimulate bone.
choices.B: Apply sustained deep compression over spinous processes.
choices.C: Favor gentle broad contact and avoid forceful pressure over fragile bony areas.
choices.D: Perform end-range spinal rotation stretches before massage.

questions.answer: Favor gentle broad contact and avoid forceful pressure over fragile bony areas.
questions.why: Bone fragility elevates injury risk with forceful localized techniques, especially over the spine and ribs. Broad, conservative pressure is safer and still therapeutic.
questions.trap: Percussion and focal compression increase fracture risk in fragile bone.

questions.prompt: A client is 10 days post abdominal surgery with steri-strips still present and asks for scar work. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Provide deep abdominal work to prevent early adhesions.
choices.B: Avoid the surgical region and request medical clearance before regional treatment.
choices.C: Stretch the scar aggressively during this visit.
choices.D: Continue normal pressure because the incision is covered.

questions.answer: Avoid the surgical region and request medical clearance before regional treatment.
questions.why: Early postoperative tissue is still healing and may not tolerate regional manipulation. Deferring local work and confirming readiness with the medical team is safest.
questions.trap: Coverage with steri-strips does not mean tissue can handle deep mobilization.

questions.prompt: A client with prior mastectomy and axillary node removal reports new heaviness and swelling in the affected arm. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Perform strong distal-to-proximal strokes on the swollen arm.
choices.B: Avoid pressure to the affected arm and refer to the oncology team or a certified lymphedema therapist.
choices.C: Use vigorous cupping to move stagnant fluid quickly.
choices.D: Continue full-body massage and ignore arm symptoms.

questions.answer: Avoid pressure to the affected arm and refer to the oncology team or a certified lymphedema therapist.
questions.why: New unilateral swelling after lymph node removal may indicate lymphedema requiring specialized management. Referral and regional caution are appropriate scope-based safety steps.
questions.trap: Aggressive fluid-moving techniques can aggravate symptoms when diagnosis and staging are unclear.

questions.prompt: During treatment, a client suddenly develops facial droop and slurred speech. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Offer water and reassess after 30 minutes.
choices.B: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).
choices.C: Continue gentle scalp massage while monitoring speech.
choices.D: Ask the client to drive to urgent care after the session.

questions.answer: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).
questions.why: Acute FAST-type neurologic changes suggest possible stroke and require immediate emergency activation. Massage must stop because this is a medical emergency.
questions.trap: Observation delays can reduce eligibility for time-sensitive stroke treatment.

questions.prompt: A client arrives smelling strongly of alcohol with impaired balance and slowed responses. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Proceed if the client signs an additional consent form.
choices.B: Shorten the session to 30 minutes and avoid deep pressure.
choices.C: Decline treatment, follow clinic policy, and arrange safe transportation as needed.
choices.D: Treat only extremities to minimize risk.

questions.answer: Decline treatment, follow clinic policy, and arrange safe transportation as needed.
questions.why: Intoxication impairs consent quality, communication, and safety monitoring during manual therapy. Deferring treatment and following policy protects client welfare.
questions.trap: A signed form does not correct impaired decision-making capacity.

questions.prompt: A client with recent lower-leg surgery reports calf pain and now sudden sharp chest pain with breathing. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Have the client breathe slowly and continue light massage.
choices.B: Massage the calf to reduce tension before reassessing chest pain.
choices.C: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).
choices.D: Recommend an urgent care visit after the appointment.

questions.answer: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).
questions.why: Recent surgery plus calf pain and pleuritic chest pain raises concern for thromboembolic emergency. Immediate emergency activation is required, not continued manual care.
questions.trap: Delaying for post-session referral may miss a life-threatening event.
questions.hook: Clot signs plus chest pain: emergency.

questions.prompt: A client has multiple ring-shaped, itchy skin lesions suspicious for a contagious fungal infection. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Massage through lotion to reduce itch and irritation.
choices.B: Cover lesions and continue full treatment.
choices.C: Avoid affected skin and postpone treatment if lesions appear contagious or widespread.
choices.D: Use friction over lesions to increase local immunity.

questions.answer: Avoid affected skin and postpone treatment if lesions appear contagious or widespread.
questions.why: Potentially contagious dermal conditions require contact precautions and often deferral, depending on extent and setting policy. This reduces transmission risk and skin aggravation.
questions.trap: Covering lesions alone does not reliably control spread risk during massage.

questions.prompt: Three days after a concussion, a client still has headache, dizziness, and light sensitivity but requests neck work. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Provide deep cervical work to break the headache cycle.
choices.B: Proceed with full treatment but avoid scalp contact.
choices.C: Defer treatment and refer for medical reassessment before massage.
choices.D: Use vigorous tapotement to improve alertness.

questions.answer: Defer treatment and refer for medical reassessment before massage.
questions.why: Persistent post-concussion symptoms indicate unresolved neurologic recovery and require medical reassessment. Massage can resume after stability and guidance are clarified.
questions.trap: Deep neck work can aggravate symptoms during an unstable recovery phase.

questions.prompt: During prone work, a client reports pounding palpitations, becomes pale, then briefly loses responsiveness and wakes confused. What is the MOST appropriate action?

choices.A: Resume treatment once the client says they feel better.
choices.B: Sit the client up quickly and monitor without calling for help.
choices.C: Stop care, monitor ABCs, and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).
choices.D: Give a sports drink and have the client walk slowly.

questions.answer: Stop care, monitor ABCs, and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).
questions.why: Transient loss of responsiveness with cardiac symptoms is an emergency requiring immediate escalation and basic safety monitoring. Continuing massage is outside scope and unsafe.
questions.trap: Symptom improvement after syncope does not rule out serious arrhythmia.

questions.prompt: Six days postpartum, a client requests leg massage but reports unilateral calf pain and new shortness of breath today. What is the BEST next step?

choices.A: Perform gentle lymphatic leg massage first, then reassess breathing.
choices.B: Provide a short relaxation massage and advise hydration.
choices.C: Treat upper body only and monitor symptoms during session.
choices.D: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).

questions.answer: Stop the session and activate emergency medical response (call 911 or local emergency services).
questions.why: Postpartum status with unilateral calf pain and dyspnea suggests possible thromboembolic emergency requiring immediate medical care. Massage is contraindicated until cleared.
questions.trap: Limiting treatment area does not mitigate potentially life-threatening systemic risk.
